title: How to Think Like Socrates: Ancient Philosophy as a Way of Life in the Modern World
author: Donald J. Robertson

chapters.title: Admitting ignorance is a strength
chapters.text:
Socrates left behind no books, yet he mastered the art of asking questions. His three foundations — probing assumptions, tolerating uncertainty, and pursuing truth — were so threatening that Athens put him to death. Millennia later, those same ideas still carry weight.

Real understanding starts when you have the nerve to say, "I don't know."

Donald J. Robertson argues that Socratic thinking may be exactly what we need in an anxious age. Whether you want to quiet irrational fears or simply sharpen your daily habits — something philosophy rarely promises in plain terms — Socrates is worth meeting on his own ground.

Wondering how ancient thought connects to real life? Stay with this summary. By the end, you'll have practical steps for thinking like Socrates today. No beard required.

chapters.title: Your life is the courtroom
chapters.text:
The year is 399 BCE. Barefoot and in his seventies, Socrates stands before a crowd of Athenians accused of impiety and corrupting youth. He opens by calling himself wise — a strange move before jurors he considers corrupt. Years earlier, his friend Chaerephon asked the Oracle at Delphi whether anyone was wiser than Socrates. The answer named Socrates himself, and the crowd groaned at the claim.

Socrates, who insisted on testing every piece of received wisdom, interviewed Athens' reputed experts and found them the most foolish of all. The lesson is direct: the Socratic method demands active thought. Most of us absorb information passively when we read or listen. Socrates asked us to run new ideas through our own judgment and values, just as he tested the Oracle's verdict. The audience read arrogance in his steady gaze and felt prosecuted themselves.

Philosophy is practiced, not memorized from textbooks. To think, in Socrates' view, is to live.

Eventually he concluded that wisdom means recognizing the limits of what we can know. Foolishness is believing our current knowledge is complete. That mindset closes us off to other experiences and ways of living, shrinking our understanding further — exactly what Socrates warned against.

He told his accusers that taking a life unjustly would burden them forever. He had long accepted mortality, so trading philosophy for freedom was unthinkable. Either way, his life as thinker and citizen would end — and we know how the story closes. The jury convicted him; the next day he drank hemlock and died.

To audit your own wisdom, imagine yourself on trial and answer honestly:

• Where did most of your time go this past year?
• What did you invest yesterday — and the days before that?
• How much did those activities actually matter to the life you want?

Did you know? People who love Greece and Greek culture are called "philhellenes."

chapters.title: Wisdom must be worked for
chapters.text:
Legend says Socrates questioned everything from the moment he could speak. Whether or not that's true, his relentless curiosity pushed him past the abstract debates common among his peers. While other philosophers mused on cosmic principles, Socrates turned toward the human mind — a path that foreshadowed modern psychotherapy in surprising ways.

The Socratic method is essentially a set of exercises that wake up the rational part of us where real knowledge lives. Rationality, in this sense, is close to what Socrates meant by wisdom. But why did he prize knowledge so highly? Consider Euthydemus, a young man obsessed with collecting wise men's writings, convinced that reading alone made him better. Today we'd call him a self-help addict.

When Socrates met him, he posed questions like: "Is lying wrong?" Obviously — until you ask whether it's just to withhold painful news from a depressed friend when you know the truth may hurt them more. Euthydemus paused. So should you.

Self-help can help sometimes, but it cannot replace your own thinking.

Testing what you consume protects you from manipulation — by media, by sales pitches, and by your own lazy assumptions.

Often that means moving through fear. People avoid what scares them — brave and timid alike. The difference: courageous people know short pain today prevents worse pain tomorrow, so they act anyway. Fearful people lack that insight; ignorance is the opposite of knowing. Ignorance hides inside sweeping generalizations, including some self-help formulas that pretend life is simple. Socratic practice reveals life's nuance. Try this exercise:

• Draw two columns labeled with opposing virtues — justice and injustice, for example.
• List clearly unjust acts in the second column: theft, murder.
• Then imagine cases where those acts might be defensible.
• Repeat with other virtues until black-and-white thinking loosens.

Through self-knowledge … men come to much good in life, and through self-deceit, they come to much harm. ~ Socrates

chapters.title: Love as the highest virtue
chapters.text:
As a boy, Socrates helped his father Sophroniscus, a sculptor, carve statues of the three Graces. While working, he wondered what gratitude and generosity really meant — and landed on a single question that would shape his life: "What is virtue?"

No philosopher of his era satisfied him. Even Anaxagoras, the leading thinker of his youth, explained feelings through what we'd now call physics and biology. Impressive for the time, yet something essential — something felt, not measured — was missing from the account.

His mother Phaenarete, a professional matchmaker who prepared young women for marriage, told him he was searching in the wrong place — or rather, among the wrong gender. She introduced him to Aspasia, a rare female philosopher in Athens. In a culture with little room for women thinkers, Socrates had never thought to seek wisdom from one. Aspasia showed him what male philosophers had overlooked: love.

Men are not born less empathetic; culture teaches them to suppress feeling.

Aspasia argued several points that reframed virtue entirely:

• Physical attraction fades as beauty ages.
• Love rooted in character and wisdom deepens as a person grows.
• To be loved authentically, build depth — perceptive people spot shallowness quickly.

Genuine love shapes character. Loving a quality in someone means wanting it for yourself — usually something good. Pursuing it is following love, for yourself and for companions, romantic or otherwise. If love means becoming better, fulfillment arrives through good action and wisdom. The Greeks called that eudaimonia — a flourishing life, not mere pleasure.

As you can see, genuine love can positively influence our character. Furthermore, loving something in a person means desiring it for ourselves. Therefore, love is wanting something we are not or do not possess, usually something good.

"But how do I practice it?" Good question — an exercise awaits at the end of this summary.

chapters.title: Words can mislead as much as inform
chapters.text:
Who would you choose — a nervous person with the right message or a great talker beating around the bush? Who wins — a nervous expert with the right facts, or a smooth talker who dodges the point? Socrates learned the answer after debating Gorgias, Athens' famous teacher of public speaking, or oratory.

Gorgias claimed oratory meant speaking well on any topic. Socrates countered that every true expert speaks well about their own field. So what separates the skilled speaker from the genuine specialist?

Nobody can master everything; breadth without depth is not expertise. A polished speaker with no real knowledge uses rhetoric to make audiences believe they practice what they preach. In their debate over teaching justice, the philosopher teaches what justice is; the orator persuades people to accept the speaker's definition of just.

Socrates warned that listeners seduced by eloquence stay ignorant while orators teach the ignorant to sound more convincing than experts before equally uninformed crowds. Nobody learns; genuine specialists go unheard.

This scenario is especially popular in politics, where a speaker takes advantage of citizens' lack of knowledge to promote a policy that benefits them. Public speaking isn't evil by nature — but stay alert when style outruns substance.

Emotion can warp clear thinking; stay calm to resist manipulation.

Cognitive distancing helps — observe your thoughts as if you were someone else:
1. Recall a recent conflict. What happened? What did you say? How did you feel?
2. Write down negative automatic thoughts.
3. Sort each into "I believe that…" and "I know that…"
4. Add a third-person frame: "[Your name] believes/knows that…" for more objectivity.

Practice this exercise regularly to easily separate your subjective thoughts from facts and notice when others try to manipulate you with their beliefs.

Emotions can distort your true thoughts; do your best to stay calm and avoid manipulation.

chapters.title: Demand evidence, not assumption
chapters.text:
Separating things you know are facts and those you believe to be true can be confusing at first. You may think: "How do I tell these meanings apart?" Let's dive deeper into it by looking at an unexpected example — death.

But first, take notice of how you speak.

First, notice your language. Manipulators serve twisted opinion as truth — but we do the same to ourselves without realizing it.

"That dog only barks at me!" or "You never help around the house!" exaggerate minor events. These overgeneralizations are one of four common cognitive distortions:

• Selective thinking: cherry-picking data out of context — remembering criticism but not praise.
• Jumping to conclusions: baseless assumptions, including "mind reading" about what others think of you.
• Exaggerating: blowing problems out of proportion or forcing binary categories — friend or enemy, nothing between.

We slip into these patterns despite our values, then fear things we barely understand. Death is a prime case.

We are not disturbed by events; our opinions about them are disturbing.

Many of us know little about death yet treat it as terrifying. Socrates said that fear rests on opinion, not evidence. Seeing the distinction makes cognitive distancing easier, easing anxiety and sharpening confidence when doubts arise.

Robertson calls the practice of building this skill "rational measurement" — replacing irrational dread with reasoned explanation. One way to apply it:

• Create columns labeled "Short-term consequences" and "Long-term consequences."
• Pick an anxious belief and weigh the cost of holding it.
• Notice when short discomfort beats years of rumination — fear of death, for instance, can keep you from living fully.

This, in return, helps avoid unnecessary anxiety and approach any doubts more confidently.

chapters.title: Practice justice to receive it
chapters.text:
Athens is synonymous with democracy, yet Socrates watched his city slide toward oligarchy during his lifetime.

Between 415 and 413 BCE, the Sicilian Expedition devastated Athens during the Peloponnesian War. Public anger rose as the Thirty Tyrants seized power, led by Critias — Socrates' student. The teacher urged his pupil, whose views on authority bordered tyranny, not to take control. He failed to stop Critias, but the episode teaches us to question our own convictions before they harden into harm.

Socrates held that a good person accepts punishment for wrongdoing with dignity. When someone feels wronged — justly or not — anger follows. Natural, yes, but anger can swamp judgment, especially when self-interest outweighs truth.

In cognitive therapy, anger often signals perceived threat. Anxiety afterward can blur what actually happened. How often do we want revenge on a critic whose point we secretly accept?

Accepting critique now prevents costlier mistakes later.

When emotions block clear thinking, Robertson suggests this exercise:

• Draw columns: "Evidence for" and "Evidence against."
• Take a core belief about injustice — "People must respect me!" — and note how disrespect feels. Uncover related beliefs beneath the surface.
• List objective evidence on both sides.
• Draft alternatives: "I'd prefer respect, but one rude person doesn't mean everyone will be."

The point of this exercise is to help you skip the emotional part and respond to unjust situations with wisdom.

While anger is natural, it can lead the person overboard, especially if they prioritize themselves over the objective truth.

Don't you see that admitting your mistakes, for the health of your own soul, is like swallowing bitter medicine for the good of your body? ~ Socrates

chapters.title: Put it into practice
chapters.text:
Socrates didn't merely discuss philosophy — he lived it. Fold his habits into your routine and watch what shifts.

• Question everything: Challenge assumptions — yours and others'. When you hear a bold claim, ask, "How do we know?" or "What's the evidence?"
• Practice intellectual humility: Each day, name one belief you might be wrong about and explore opposing views with genuine curiosity.
• Hold Socratic dialogue: Pick a deep question — "What is justice?" or "What makes a good life?" — and discuss it with a friend. Aim for insight, not victory.
• Examine your beliefs: List five core convictions about life, morality, or success. For each, ask why you hold it and whether the opposite could be true.
• Embrace productive ignorance: When you don't know something, say so — then learn. "I don't know, but I'd like to find out" opens doors beyond your usual interests.

Let curiosity about what you haven't discovered yet — especially about your own mind — be your guide.

Read it till the end?
